title: Why Spiritual Awakening Often Leads to Changes in Your Relationship with Time
meta_description: Your spiritual awakening is not breaking time but revealing its true nature. You are shifting from clock time to soul time. This is not dysfunction. This is expansion. Trust the new rhythm.
og_title: Why Spiritual Awakening Often Leads to Changes in Your Relationship with Time
og_description: Your spiritual awakening is not breaking time but revealing its true nature. You are shifting from clock time to soul time. This is not dysfunction. This is expansion. Trust the new rhythm.
body:
You’re Not Losing Time - You’re Feeling It Differently

There’s this moment that happens, usually when you least expect it. You’re sitting in your living room, or driving to work, or staring at a clock that says 3:47 PM. And something shifts. Time doesn’t feel like it used to. It stretches. It collapses. It feels thick and slow, then thin and fast. You start to wonder if you’re going crazy, or if your watch is broken, or if everyone else is just pretending that time makes sense.

You’re not crazy. Your watch isn’t broken. And you’re not the only one feeling this.

When you go through a spiritual awakening, your relationship with time changes because your nervous system is recalibrating. You’re not just “noticing time more.” You’re literally experiencing it on a different frequency. The linear, tick-tock world you grew up in starts to feel like a cage you’ve outgrown. And that can be disorienting as hell. Right?!

I’ve sat with dozens of people who describe this exact thing. They say things like: “I feel like I’m living in two different timelines at once.” Or: “I can’t keep up with the 9-to-5 anymore because my body doesn’t agree with the hours.” Or: “I keep having these moments where it feels like I’m inside the past and the future at the same time, and I don’t know how to explain it to anyone.”

Let me tell you something straight: nothing is wrong with you. Your nervous system is calibrated for a wider range than most people. You’re picking up on the fact that time isn’t a straight line - it’s a field. And when you start sensing that field, the clock on the wall stops being the authority it used to be.

Why Your Awakening Messes With Your Internal Clock

Your spiritual awakening doesn’t just change what you believe about God, the universe, or your purpose. It changes how your body experiences reality. And time is a huge part of that reality.

Before awakening, most of us live by the external clock. You wake up when the alarm says to. You eat when the lunch break says to. You sleep when the evening routine says to. Your whole life is organized around a system that treats time like a resource to be managed, optimized, and squeezed dry. That system works fine - until it doesn’t.

When your awakening kicks in, your internal rhythm starts talking louder. It says things like: “I’m tired at 9 PM, not 11 PM.” Or: “I need to sit in silence for an hour before I can do anything.” Or: “That project deadline feels fake to my soul.” Your body starts rejecting the tyranny of the clock. And that’s when the real discomfort begins.

You start noticing that some days fly by in what feels like minutes, while other hours stretch into eternities. You might find yourself staring at a sunset for what feels like an hour, only to check your phone and realize ten minutes passed. Or you might sit down to meditate for twenty minutes and emerge feeling like you were gone for three days.

This isn’t a glitch. It’s a feature of your awakening. You’re shifting from experiencing time as a container (something you move through) to experiencing time as a fluid (something that moves through you). Does that land?

The Collapse of Past, Present, and Future

One of the biggest shifts I see in people going through this is that the boundaries between past, present, and future start to dissolve. You might have a memory that feels as vivid as right now. Or you might have a premonition about something that hasn’t happened yet, and it feels like it already has. That’s not imagination. That’s your awareness opening up to the fact that time isn’t a line - it’s a loop, a spiral, a field of all possibilities.

I remember a client telling me she couldn’t stop crying at random moments because she’d feel a grief from when she was seven years old, and it would hit her like it was happening in the present. She thought she was broken. She was actually healing. Her nervous system was finally allowing her to feel that old pain in a safe way, and it was collapsing the distance between then and now.

Another client told me he kept seeing images of his future self - an older version of him, calm and wise, living in a house by the water. He was terrified he was making it up. But the more he trusted those glimpses, the more his present choices started aligning with that future. He wasn’t predicting the future. He was sensing a timeline that already existed, and his awareness was tuning into it.

This is what spiritual awakening does to time: it shows you that everything is happening at once. And that can feel overwhelming if you’re still trying to live by the clock. But it can also be liberating if you let it.

"Time is not a line. It's a field. And when you stop trying to walk the line, you start feeling the field. That's when your life becomes less about managing hours and more about trusting the rhythm."

Why Linear Time Feels Like a Cage Now

If you’re in the middle of an awakening, you’ve probably noticed that the standard 9-to-5 work schedule, the rush-hour commute, the constant pressure to be productive - it all feels like a violation. You might feel physically ill when you think about spending forty hours a week doing something that doesn’t connect to your soul. You might feel rage when someone tells you to “hurry up” or “be realistic” about how long things take.

That’s not laziness. That’s your system saying: “I cannot live inside this artificial structure anymore.” Your soul is designed to flow, not to be crammed into a schedule that ignores your natural cycles.

Think about it this way: before clocks, humans lived by the sun, the moon, the seasons, and their own bodies. You woke when you were rested. You worked when you had energy. You rested when you were tired. You celebrated when the harvest came. Your whole life was synchronized with natural rhythms. Then industrialization came along and told you that your body’s rhythms were inefficient. You were trained to ignore your own signals and obey the clock instead.

Spiritual awakening is the process of un-training that. It’s your soul saying: “I remember what real time feels like. And this isn’t it.” So of course you feel out of sync. You’re re-syncing with a deeper rhythm. That takes time - or rather, it takes a different relationship with time.

How to Survive (and Thrive) When Time Feels Unstable

So what do you do when you’re in this weird in-between space, where the old clock doesn’t work and the new rhythm isn’t clear yet? You don’t try to force it. You don’t try to “fix” your relationship with time. You learn to ride it.

Here are a few things that have helped me and the people I work with:

Stop Fighting the Stretches

When time feels slow, don’t try to speed it up. When it feels fast, don’t try to slow it down. Just notice it. Say to yourself: “Oh, time is stretching right now. That’s interesting.” Or: “Time is moving fast. I wonder what that’s about.” The moment you stop resisting the experience, it becomes less scary. You’re not broken. You’re just in a different tempo.

Let Your Body Lead

Your body knows what time it needs. If you feel hungry at 10 AM instead of noon, eat. If you feel tired at 8 PM instead of 10, go to sleep. If you feel a surge of energy at 3 AM, get up and do something creative. Yes, this might mess with your schedule. But your schedule was probably built on someone else’s clock anyway. Trust your body’s signals more than the numbers on a screen.

Create Time Anchors

When time feels disorienting, it helps to have a few anchors - small rituals that ground you in the present moment. Maybe it’s lighting a candle at the same time each day. Maybe it’s a morning walk where you notice the light. Maybe it’s a breathing exercise you do before meals. These anchors don’t force time to be linear. They just give you a touchpoint in the flow. If you’re looking for a practical guide to building these anchors, I recommend checking out The Power of Now by Eckhart Tolle on Amazon (paid link). It’s a classic for a reason - it helps you stay present when time feels like a rollercoaster.

Honor Your Cycles

You’re not a machine. You’re a living being with cycles. Some days you’ll be productive. Some days you’ll be still. Some weeks you’ll feel like you’re moving through molasses. Some months you’ll feel like you’re flying. That’s not a problem. That’s nature. Honor it. If you’re in a slow cycle, don’t beat yourself up for not being fast. If you’re in a fast cycle, don’t guilt yourself for not resting. Flow with what is.

Talk to Someone Who Gets It

This is a big one. When you’re in the thick of a time-shift, it helps to have someone who understands what you’re going through. Not someone who tells you to “just manage your schedule better.” Someone who gets that time is not what you thought it was. If you don’t have that person in your life yet, consider finding a community or a mentor who works with spiritual awakening. You don’t have to do this alone.

The Gift of Timelessness

Here’s the thing nobody tells you about this shift: eventually, the disorientation settles. And what’s left is something beautiful. You start to experience moments of timelessness - where you’re so fully present that the clock disappears entirely. You’re not worried about the past. You’re not anxious about the future. You’re just here. And in those moments, you realize that time was never the problem. The problem was that you were trying to live outside the present moment.

When you’re in a state of timelessness, everything slows down. You notice details you never saw before - the texture of a leaf, the sound of your breath, the warmth of the sun on your skin. And yet, paradoxically, you also feel like you have all the time in the world. You’re not rushed. You’re not waiting. You’re just being.

This is the gift that spiritual awakening offers you: not a better schedule, but a deeper relationship with the now. And once you taste that, you can never go back to the old way of living. You might still use a clock. You might still have deadlines. But you’ll know that those are just tools, not the truth. The truth is that you are timeless, and you always have been.

If you’re struggling with this transition, I want you to know that it’s normal. It’s part of the process. You’re not losing your mind. You’re losing your attachment to a limited view of time. And that’s a good thing, even if it feels uncomfortable right now. For more on navigating this shift, you might find The Untethered Soul by Michael A. Singer on Amazon (paid link) helpful - it talks directly to the part of you that’s trying to hold on when everything is changing.

Practical Tools for the Time-Shift

Let me give you a few more concrete things you can do when time feels like it’s slipping away or stretching too thin:

Use a Timer for Practice, Not for Pressure

Set a timer for five minutes and just sit with your eyes closed. Don’t try to meditate. Just notice how time feels. Does it feel long? Short? Sticky? Loose? Just observe. This trains your awareness to be with time instead of fighting it.

Write Down Your Time Experiences

Keep a journal of moments when time felt weird. Write down what was happening, how you felt, and what you noticed. Over time, you’ll start to see patterns. Maybe time stretches when you’re anxious. Maybe it collapses when you’re deeply focused. The patterns will help you understand your own rhythm.

Practice “No Time” Days

Pick one day a week where you don’t wear a watch, don’t check your phone for the time, and don’t schedule anything. Just let the day unfold. This is terrifying for a lot of people, but it’s also incredibly healing. You’ll learn that you can trust your own internal sense of time more than you think.

Read Something That Expands Your View of Time

There are some beautiful books out there that talk about time from a spiritual perspective. One that really shifted my understanding is The Celestine Prophecy by James Redfield on Amazon (paid link). It’s not a textbook - it’s a story that opens your mind to the idea that time is more flexible than we’ve been taught.

When Your Relationship with Time Changes, Everything Changes

I want you to hear this clearly: your changing relationship with time is not a side effect of your awakening. It is a core part of it. You are being invited to live in a different way - not by the clock, but by the presence. And that invitation is terrifying and beautiful at the same time.

You might lose some things when you accept this invitation. You might lose the comfort of a predictable schedule. You might lose friends who don’t understand why you can’t “just show up on time” anymore. You might lose the sense of control that came from believing you could manage every minute of your life. But what you gain is so much bigger: you gain the ability to be fully alive in every moment, without the constant pressure of past and future weighing on you.

That’s the real freedom of spiritual awakening. It’s not about escaping time. It’s about realizing that you were never trapped in it to begin with. You were just dreaming that you were. And now you’re waking up.

If you’re ready to explore this further, I’ve put together a short assessment that can help you understand where you are in your awakening process and what your next steps might be. It’s called the Spiritual Awakening Quiz, and it’s designed to give you clarity when everything feels fuzzy. You can take it here. No pressure - just a tool to help you see the path ahead a little more clearly.

And if you need something to read while you’re in the thick of it, I’d recommend A New Earth by Eckhart Tolle on Amazon (paid link). It’s a deep dive into how awakening changes your relationship with everything - including time. It helped me when I was in the middle of my own time-shift, and it might help you too.

You’re not losing time. You’re finding a deeper way to live inside it. And that’s a beautiful thing, even when it feels hard. Keep going. You’re right on time.
